speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEPING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Date-- January 25, 1996. Survivor-- Kaz Wolff-Zdzienicki. Interviewer-- Padigail Meskin. The city of Byrne Village, Natal, South Africa. Language-- English. My name is Padigail Meskin. I am interviewing Kaz Wolff-Zdzienicki on the 25th of January, 1996, in the village of Byrne in Natal, South Africa. Kaz? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right. Kaz, would you please tell us your full name? And could you spell the name for-- the names for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [INAUDIBLE] I am Polish-born, and I am still Polish, despite my South African citizenship. And if you don't mind, I will spell full name in Polish. It's mean, first names-- I am rich. I got three. Kazimierz [? Sigmund ?] Jan. And the family name is [? double-barrel ?] name, Wolff, W-O-L-F-F, stroke, Zdzienicki, Z-D-Z-I-E-N-I-C-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you tell us where you were born, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Warsaw, in Poland, in, on 4th of May, 1922, a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Quite a long time ago. Could you please tell us a little bit about your early life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was a businessman, a rather, I would say, a wealthy man. I had a beautiful house, a loving mother, loving father. I had a younger brother. And we were living in Warsaw. And first I joined a gymnasium, which means a high school, in Warsaw, name of Rej. Rej was a Polish poet in the middle centuries, say 15th century. And then I was sent to a, for a, I would say, more [? disciplinated ?] education through the Jesuit college, southeast Poland, where I was for last two years before the outbreak of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: In just prior to the, to the war breaking out, were you aware of any rise in Nazism? Did Nazism have any kind of effect on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in, on the beginning of the war, after about six months of the war, I lost my parents. And I was left with my younger brother. I was at this time 17. My brother was 15. We had at home joined us my grandmother, the mother of my mother. And so but thanks to [INAUDIBLE], still one of the companies of my father was in operation, we had the material background to survive. I have done matric in 1940 straight away after the outbreak, after coming back from East Poland, where we escaped before the German. We came back afterwards. And afterwards, I joined the special faculty of medicine [INAUDIBLE], working at the same time in a hospital. And in 1940, I was approached by one of my school colleagues to join an underground. And as I had matric, I was incorporated into the, of course, clandestine officer's school. Due to the fact that I spoke fluently German, I was put into intelligence. I was instructed to catch contact with the German officers to receive information, to collect information, what's going on in the army, especially after the outbreak of the war with Russia. You must understand, it was impossible, for example, to destroy all the trains going to the east front. So it was, it have to be very selective. So it was important to know what kind of goods are most important for the front. And this, you could have only from the officers or from the people coming back from the east front.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I mixed a little bit among them from one to another, introduced by one and to the other one. And so I organized me, I would say, a net of informer among the officers coming back from east front. One gave my address or telephone number to another one and say, well, when you come to Warsaw, we've got a friend, Mr. Wolff. It was a German family name. So they, they had been not aware I am Polish or I am German or what I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: He take you home. He give you a good food and a drink and this. And this way, I was working and collecting the information and afterwards, pass my information to the higher level to my, I would say, controller, how you call it, in, in this kind of job. And he gave the instruction to a proper group of partisan in the country what kind of trains should be destroyed on their way to their front. Well, this was my job. And by the way, I was as well instructed in this officer's training, I would say. So, so I arrived to a rank of warrant officer. And on the 25th of February, I was arrested by the Gestapo. [? On the other-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, what year-- what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. And it was a funny story, you know, because I came back from the hospital about three o'clock for lunch. And just after 3:00, I received a phone call in Polish, somebody who approached me with my, say, official professional language title, doctor, said, oh, Dr. Wolff, I want to see you. I said, well, what I want, what you want? I want to see you at home if possible, as soon as possible. I say, OK, but please, before four o'clock. Because I had a four o'clock date with a very pretty girl. So I don't wanted to stay at home. I understood it's one of the patients from the hospital, and as I was working at this time in the emergency room. So it's possible somebody wanted talk about himself or about family or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about 10 to 4:00, we had a big flat with two entrances, front entrance and kitchen entrance. And I heard up front steps going through the flat into my study, where I was sitting after dinner, after lunch. And there arrived two civilian Gestapo men. They asked me, are you Kazimierz Wolff-Zdzienicki? I say, yeah. Hand up! And then I was arrested straight away.

speaker: interviewee
text: How I was arrested, afterwards I found out. I had a messenger, a liaison messenger, between me and my superior. And this was a young chap. He was maybe 16 or 17. And when he was arrested, and they gave him a hard time, the Gestapo, from whom he collected information and to whom he brought the information, well, he gave my name and my address. And so it was. And then I was put in the Gestapo prison, of course. I was interrogated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the Gestapo prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gestapo prison was in Warsaw. It was Pawiak, where there was a street named Pawia. And was a Pawiak. It was in the center of the, of the ghetto. So going to the interrogation, which was on the infamous Szucha Street in Warsaw, and back, you pass always by-- by the gate, or you saw the people in the street and so on. And well, I will not tell you was going on in the Gestapo interrogation, you know. It's a known story, you see. You receive a hiding. You receive a beating. You receive a-- I got still couple of burns on my chest from this time. But I remember, it was end of April, 1943, when started the uprising of the ghetto in Warsaw. There was a shooting all around, you know, and plenty of SS, German, Ukrainian. The Russian army of General Vlasov was involved and some other, I would say, cossacks and so on. And, well, even I wrote a memoir about this, shot outside the bars and beyond the bars. We saw from our window what's happened a little bit, because it was forbidden to look through the windows. But we saw. One day, I remember there was all plenty of smoke all around of burned houses. And, [BACKGROUND NOISE] and then they brought a big group of Jews just before the entrance of the Gestapo prison. They are surrounded by the SS people. They brought them-- they divided them in, I would say, section of 20, 30. They brought them into the big house opposite in the yard behind the houses. And we heard was a shooting. Da-da-da-da-da. And the SS came out, has taken the second group. It was women, children, different ages, you know, men. Again, in the doorway, deep in the yard, and again the shooting. And so it was a couple of time, you know. It was just, I would say, two days-- this fact happened about two days before we left for Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the Gestapo camp-- in the Gestapo prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in this prison, I was two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they interrogating you right the way through?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then on 29th of April, they brought us in the morning into the trucks who arrive in the yard of the prison, loaded on the trucks, and to the railway station on the-- it was not the main railway station of Warsaw. But it was on outskirt of Warsaw-- loaded into the cattle trucks about 50, 60 for each cattle truck. And on the way between the prison and the railway station, we saw the burned houses and plenty of corpses on the street in the ghetto, killed people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we arrive on the 29 of April, '43 to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, before we go--

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was not the main camp. It was the Auschwitz-Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, just before we go to Auschwitz-Birkenau, when you were actually transported, were you transported in-- in trucks? Or were you on a railway?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the railway cattle trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Railway truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it, were you transported with Jews or non-Jews, or what did know-- have any idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We are not aware where we are going, which direction. We understood, looking on the, through the small windows, on the station, on the road, that we are going southwest. But where? We were not aware.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you didn't know you were going to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And eventually, I would say one hour before we landed in Auschwitz, one of the prisoners, who was in Auschwitz who escaped from the camp and was re-arrested, he say, yes, I know. I recognize. We are going to Auschwitz. We arrive on a ramp, offloading ramp. And they told us to disembark, you know. And we came to the camp. And it was night. And we receive nothing to eat during the day, nothing to eat--

speaker: interviewer
text: During the transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not even water.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was from about eight o'clock in the morning till about nine, 10 o'clock evenings, so even no water.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no sanitation?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived to the concentration camp, we put, they put us in-- we marched about three, three kilometers from the offloading ramp. This time was not ramp as is today in Birkenau in the middle of the camp. It was outside. We saw only the big chimneys, plenty of fire and smoke going out. It was-- afterwards, I realized it was a crematorium working at night. But we are, I, I personally accepted this. It's, oh, a bakery is working-- for the beginning, you know, my first impression was. And then early morning, arrive people who have taken our particulars, you know, and filled the records for each of us. And then, of course, they put a tattoo on the arm. And there was only one concentration camp who puts tattoo with a number on your arm. You know, it was due to the fact that we had no ID papers on us. And to know, and they-- it was a such a quantity of people dying every day that it was impossible to keep a proper record, who is alive and who is dead. So the only way to keep a record was to numbered us. On the beginning, they told to-- before I arrived, of course, in '42, '41-- they had to paint their number on the front. But at work or in the rain, this all completely was destroyed. So they found out that the best chance, it was they put the number. So they put the number on the arm. Well, and then arrive the chief prisoner, who was, I would say, the chief prisoner of the camp. And he had a short speech to us and told us, it's not a sanatorium. It's not a hotel. It's concen-- it's, there is no mother, no father, no God. I am the god here. You are in concentration camp, Birkenau-Auschwitz. After such a talk, we realized that we are really not in a sanitorium. And the story will be rather hard for us. Well, we arrived. It was a group of 406, all Polish, all from the underground from different ages. I was one of the youngsters. I was 20 when I arrived to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: After a couple of days, they asked me-- sorry. Excuse me. You will cut off. Anyhow, after a couple of days, they asked people if maybe is any doctor here. There was no doctor. Maybe student of medicine? So I reported myself. I was student of medicine. I was in the third year at this time. And I had my experience working whole time in the hospital on the surgical department. So I was transferred as a-- they called us [INAUDIBLE] doctor. It's a, no, it was a second-hand, second-rung doctor, is a younger doctor in the hospital. I was really working like a nursemaid during the day or during the night, except for the one or two hours after the roll-call on the evening, when I was working in this, I would say, ambulatory, you know, taking, checking the patient and changing dressings and so on and so on. So it was. And I remember on the, as I mentioned in the beginning, I was born on the 4th of May, 1922. And it was arrived the day, after five days, it was my birthday. I finished 21. And I was just on the night duty in the hospital barrack, sitting at the table. And I was very sad. I never expected that I will have my 21st birthday in such a situation, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And well, my job was during the night really not pleasant, you know. I had no medicine to give to the patient as it was at this time, no medicine at all, almost nothing except for aspirin for the patient. So they have been dying terribly. And my job with my colleague, who was a Frenchman, was to take the corpses out from the shelves, I would say, and take it out. That was my job during the night duty in the hospital barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was sitting at a table and at very moment arrived, walked in, a much older-- not so much with age, but with standing in the camp. He was already for more than one year in the camp. A male nurse come, approached us. It was about nine o'clock evening, so after the night roll-call, after the taking out, you see. The, the lights were off already in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And looked on me and say, Kaz, why are you so sad? And I explained him, well, I am finishing 21 today. I never expected to-- he say, OK. I am sorry for you. He walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a minute, he arrived with a birthday gift. It was the most beautiful birthday gift in my life. He gave me half a loaf of bread. And I will remember to the end of my days this half loaf of bread for my 21st birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful gift. Could you please describe-- could you give me a bit of a description about the hospitals? You said it was like shelves. Were there no proper beds and things like that? How did they set it up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had couple of barracks. There was two where they had really shelves. There was a-- how do you call it in English-- [NON-ENGLISH]? No, there was a little bit some blankets on which they have been put in. It was a very, very difficult and very horrible condition, you know. You saw sometime, there are photograph from the concentration camp. I was lucky, because I was, due to the fact that I was working as a [? health ?] doctor, I was on duty attached to the block number 12. And block number 12 was, I would say, a VIP block. There was three-story beds. And this was better condition there, a little better. But of course, there was no civilized condition, you know. Forget about it. Especially, there was no medicine. Mr. Mengele, who was in charge of the hospital medical service that was all Auschwitz, arrived from time to time and checked. He never approached the patient in the bed. He checked only the card of the patient hanging outside the bed. If it was written [? grippe, ?] which mean flu, with question mark, he realized very quick that it is a typhoid. And everybody, doesn't matter it was a Jew or Polish or Russian or French, once you got typhoid, the black fever, was put to the gas chamber. So Mr. Mengele never approached. When it was grippe, he put his bed number in his notebook. And evenings, he arrive again to supervise the loading of the people condemned to the gas chamber according to the bed number. I was lucky. I was, I had typhoid. And I was in his notebook, but I was unconscious. But to a certain extent, I was lucky, because a patient, a prisoner who was on the top bed-- and I was at the ground bed-- died during the day. And my colleagues change, put the corpse at the bottom and me on top. When Mengele arrived in the evening, he was told, and that one, that one died during the day. Good. OK. And so I was lucky to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever work with Mengele at all in any of his experiments, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No. I know that he have done the experiment. But experiment have been done always in Auschwitz one, in the main camp. And I was Birkenau. We had the four crematoriums, and this is, this was our death factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just-- you said that you, you actually stayed in, in block 12. Were you living there? Or were you working there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living there, and I was working there. There was a certain section for the male nurse on the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there only men there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only men. The women were in a separate section?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- the camp was divided. Women's camp was completely different section and men completely different.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you collected the bodies, what did you do with them? Did you take them-- were you personally responsible?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we, we put them outside, you know. And in the morning, arrived a truck. And we loaded these corpses on the truck and to crematorium from--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew by then where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you actually realize that there was a gas chamber in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, after 24 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew very--

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew very well. We knew very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were aware of the-- and then you see, once they finished, we had four crematoriums with a 24 hours capacity of 36,000. But I remember the period of June 1944, when arrived the big transport from Hungarian ghettos of Hungarian Jews. And they-- there arrived more than 36,000 today. So there was a villa, a house, in the forest, in the birch forest behind the crematorium, which they transformed into a gas chamber. And then they digged big trenches. And when they gassed the people in the-- we called this white villa-- in the white villa, white house, then we, they put the layer of corpses, wood, corpses, wood, diesel in on, and fire. And it was burning like that. Is that it? And then I remember one story from this time, from 1944, arrived many of transport. But when they arrive at transport, and they sent prisoners always to clean the trucks from the luggage, suitcases, and corpses. And among them, they found two children. Already, the people went away. They loaded the corpses on the truck to take to crematorium. And they have been instructed to put the children as well on top. And the children, the two, one girl of about three, four, and same age a young, little boy of [? four or ?] five, had been sitting on the corpses, seeing us. They greeted us with hands and smiling to us. And they arrived to the big ditches with fire. They tipped the truck. And like snow that fell, the children came to the fire.
